subject: Chemistry
q:
Following figure shows spectrum of an ideal black body at four different temperatures. The number of correct statement/s from the following is _______.

A. T4 > T3 > T2 > T1
 B. The black body consists of particles performing simple harmonic motion.
 C. The peak of the spectrum shifts to shorter wavelength as temperature increases.
 D. T1ν1 = T2ν2 = T3ν3
 E. The given spectrum could be explained using quantisation of energy
question:
Following figure shows spectrum of an ideal black body at four different temperatures. The number of correct statement/s from the following is _______.

A. T4 > T3 > T2 > T1
 B. The black body consists of particles performing simple harmonic motion.
 C. The peak of the spectrum shifts to shorter wavelength as temperature increases.
 D. T1ν1 = T2ν2 = T3ν3
 E. The given spectrum could be explained using quantisation of energy
solution: The spectrum of Black body radiation is explained using quantization of energy. With increase in temperature, peak of spectrum shifts to shorter wavelength or higher frequency. Thus, for above graph T1 > T2 > T3 > T4

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers is not allowed?
question: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers is not allowed?
solution:
The possible values of principal quantum number 'n' can be any positive integer.

For a particular value of 'n', the possible values of azimuthal quantum number is from 0 to n-1.

Thus, for n=3, the possible 'l' values are 0, 1, 2.

The possible values of magnetic quantum number 'm' is from '-l' to '+l'.

Thus, for l=2, the possible values of 'm' are -2, -1, 0, +1, +2.

The possible values of spin quantum number 's' is -12 and +12.

In option C.

⇒ l=3, not possible for n=3

subject: Chemistry
q: The work functions of two metals $\left(M_{A}\right.$ and $\left.M_{B}\right)$ are in the $1: 2$ ratio. When these metals are exposed to photons of energy 6 eV, the kinetic energy of liberated electrons of $M_{A}: M_{B}$ is in the ratio of $2.642: 1$. The work functions (in eV) of $M_{A}$ and $M_{B}$ are respectively.
question: The work functions of two metals $\left(M_{A}\right.$ and $\left.M_{B}\right)$ are in the $1: 2$ ratio. When these metals are exposed to photons of energy 6 eV, the kinetic energy of liberated electrons of $M_{A}: M_{B}$ is in the ratio of $2.642: 1$. The work functions (in eV) of $M_{A}$ and $M_{B}$ are respectively.
solution:
The work functions satisfy $W_A : W_B = 1:2$.
Let $W_A = W$ and $W_B = 2W$.
Using the photoelectric equation
$KE = h\nu - W$, we have $KE_A = 6 - W$ and $KE_B = 6 - 2W$.
Given the kinetic energy ratio $\frac{KE_A}{KE_B} = 2.642$, we get $\frac{6-W}{6-2W} = 2.642$.
Solving: $6 - W = 2.642(6 - 2W)$ gives $6 - W = 15.852 - 5.284W$
So $4.284W = 9.852$ and $W = 2.3$ eV.
Thus $W_A = 2.3$ eV and $W_B = 4.6$ eV.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The electron in the nth  orbit of Li2+  is excited to (n+1) orbit using the radiation of energy 1.47×10-17 J (as shown in the diagram). The value of n is ___________

Given : RH=2.18×10-18 J
question:
The electron in the nth  orbit of Li2+  is excited to (n+1) orbit using the radiation of energy 1.47×10-17 J (as shown in the diagram). The value of n is ___________

Given : RH=2.18×10-18 J
solution:
The difference energy between two energy levels is 

∆E=2.18×10-18 1n12-1n22 J

1.47×10-17=2.18×10-18×(9)1(n)2-1(n+1)2

0.749=1n2-1(n+1)2

n = 1

Hence, the transition is taking place between 1→2, and it comes under Lymann series.

If we take 0.749≃0.75=34

q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : It is impossible to specify simultaneously with arbitrary precision, both the linear momentum and the position of a particle.
Statement (II) : If the uncertainty in the measurement of position and uncertainty in measurement of momentum are equal for an electron, then the uncertainty in the measurement of velocity is $\geqslant \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : It is impossible to specify simultaneously with arbitrary precision, both the linear momentum and the position of a particle.
Statement (II) : If the uncertainty in the measurement of position and uncertainty in measurement of momentum are equal for an electron, then the uncertainty in the measurement of velocity is $\geqslant \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
According to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle, it is impossible to determine simultaneously the exact position and momentum of particle like electron
If 
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta p=\Delta x \\ & \Delta p \cdot \Delta x \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi} \\ &(\Delta p)^2 \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi} \\ & \Delta p \geq \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & m \Delta v \geq \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & \Delta v \geq \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2 m}\end{aligned}$

q: The wavelength of the radiation emitted, when in hydrogen atom electron falls from infinity to stationary state 1 , would be (Rydberg constant $\left.=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\right)$
question: The wavelength of the radiation emitted, when in hydrogen atom electron falls from infinity to stationary state 1 , would be (Rydberg constant $\left.=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\right)$
solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]$
$=1.097 \times 10^7\left(\frac{1}{1}\right)$
$\lambda=\frac{1}{1.097} \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$

solution:
hν-ϕ=KE

hcλincident=KE+ϕ

Given ϕ<<KE

hcλincident≈KE

hcλincident=p22m ......(i)

hcλ'=1.5p22m …….(ii)

Divide (i) and (ii)

λ'λ=p22.25p2

λ'=λ×1002.25=49

λ'=49λ

q:
The wave function Ψ of 2 s is given by

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r=r0, radial node is formed. Thus, r0 in terms of a0
question:
The wave function Ψ of 2 s is given by

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r=r0, radial node is formed. Thus, r0 in terms of a0
solution:
The wave function (Ψ) of 2s is given by,

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r = r0, radial node is formed.

Ψ2s2 = Probability density of finding an electron

At node, Ψ2s2 = 0 (∵Probability of finding an electron is zero at the node)

Ψ2s2 =122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a02

⇒ 0 =18π1a0 2-r0a02e-2r0/a0

⇒ 2 - r0a0 = 0

⇒ 2 = r0a0

⇒ 2a0 = r0

So, the correct option is D.

question:

is the boundary surface diagram of the $2 p_x$ orbital.

solution:
(A) Azimuthal quantum number $(\ell)$ indicates the shape of orbital occupied by the electron
(B)

$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}$ orbital
(C) The + and - sign in the wave function of $2 p_x$ orbital refer to the sign (Phase) of the wave function, not the charge
(D) The wave function of $2 p_x$ orbital will be zero in yz plane (Nodal plane).

subject: Chemistry
q: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, which of the following statement is incorrect?
question: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, which of the following statement is incorrect?
options: Radius of \(3^{\text {rd }}\) orbit is nine times larger than that of \(1^{\text {st }}\) orbit.
Radius of \(8^{\text {th }}\) orbit is four times larger than that of \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit.
Radius of \(6^{\text {th }}\) orbit is three time larger than that of \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit.
Radius of \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit is four times larger than that of \(2^{\text {nd }}\) orbit.
solution:
\(\mathrm{r} \propto \mathrm{n}^2\)
(1) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_3}{\mathrm{r}_1}=\frac{9}{1}\)
(2) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_8}{\mathrm{r}_4}=\frac{64}{16}=4\)
(3) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_6}{\mathrm{r}_4}=\left(\frac{6}{4}\right)^2=\frac{9}{4}\)
(4) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_4}{\mathrm{r}_2}=\left(\frac{4}{2}\right)^2=4\)

$$

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> are charge and mass of an electron, respectively, then the value of <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> (where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> is wavelength associated with the electron wave) is given by:

subject: Chemistry
q:
The surface of sodium metal is irradiated with radiation of wavelength $x$ nm. The kinetic energy of ejected electrons is $2.8 \times 10^{-20}$ J. The work function of sodium is $2.3$ eV. The value of $x$ is _____ $\times 10^2$ nm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34}$ J s; $1$ eV $= 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J; $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m s$^{-1}$)
question:
The surface of sodium metal is irradiated with radiation of wavelength $x$ nm. The kinetic energy of ejected electrons is $2.8 \times 10^{-20}$ J. The work function of sodium is $2.3$ eV. The value of $x$ is _____ $\times 10^2$ nm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34}$ J s; $1$ eV $= 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J; $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m s$^{-1}$)
solution:
The work function of sodium in Joules is:

$\Phi = 2.3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 3.68 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The kinetic energy of the ejected electrons is:

$K = 2.8 \times 10^{-20} = 0.28 \times 10^{-19}$ J

Using the photoelectric equation, the energy of the incident photon is:

$E = \Phi + K$

$E = 3.68 \times 10^{-19} + 0.28 \times 10^{-19} = 3.96 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The wavelength of the incident radiation is calculated using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E} = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^8}{3.96 \times 10^{-19}}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{19.8 \times 10^{-26}}{3.96 \times 10^{-19}} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m

Converting the wavelength to nanometers:

$\lambda = 500 \times 10^{-9}$ m $= 500$ nm

Given that the wavelength is $x$ nm, we get $x = 500$.

Expressing $x$ in the required format:

$x = 5 \times 10^2$

The value to be filled in the blank is $5$.

Answer: $5$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are the quantum numbers for 4 electrons.

A. n=3,l=2,m1=1,ms=+12

B. n=4,l=1,m1=0,ms=+12

C. n=4,l=2,m1=-2,ms=-12

D. n=3,l=1,m1=-1,ms=+12

The correct order of increasing energy is
question:
Given below are the quantum numbers for 4 electrons.

A. n=3,l=2,m1=1,ms=+12

B. n=4,l=1,m1=0,ms=+12

C. n=4,l=2,m1=-2,ms=-12

D. n=3,l=1,m1=-1,ms=+12

The correct order of increasing energy is
solution:
Energy order of sub-shell decided by n+l value.

Greater the n+l value of a sub-shell greater is its energy.

If two sub-shells have same n+l values the orbital with greater principal quantum number has greater energy

A⇒3 d⇒n+l=3+2=5

B⇒4p⇒n+l=4+1=5

C⇒4 d⇒n+l=4+2=6

D⇒3s⇒n+l=3+0=3

D<A<B<C

subject: Chemistry
q: Identify the incorrect statement from the following.
question: Identify the incorrect statement from the following.
options: A circular path around the nucleus in which an electron moves is proposed as Bohr's orbit.
An orbital is the one electron wave function
The existence of Bohr's orbits is supported by hydrogen spectrum.

Atomic orbital is characterised by the quantum numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> only
solution: An electron can move only in those orbits for which its angular momentum is integral multiple of h2π. That means angular momentum is quantised. Radiation is emitted or absorbed only when transition of electron takes place from one quantised value of angular momentum to another. Therefore. Maxwell's electromagnetic theory does not apply here that is why only certain fixed orbits are allowed. Atomic orbital is characterised by n,l,m. So, the option D is incorrect.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The following sets of quantum numbers represent four electrons in an atom.
(i) $n=4, l=1$
(ii) $n=4, l=0$
(iii) $n=3, l=2$
(iv) $n=3, l=1$
The sequence representing increasing order of energy, is
question:
The following sets of quantum numbers represent four electrons in an atom.
(i) $n=4, l=1$
(ii) $n=4, l=0$
(iii) $n=3, l=2$
(iv) $n=3, l=1$
The sequence representing increasing order of energy, is
solution:
(i) $4 p$
(ii) $4 \mathrm{~s}$
(iii) $3 d$
(iv) $3 p$
According to Bohr Bury's $(n+\ell)$ rule, increasing order of energy will be (iv)  < (ii) $ < $ (iii) $ < $ (i).
Note: If the two orbitals have same value of $(n+\ell)$ then the orbital with lower value of $n$ will be filled first.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is correct set of $4$ quantum numbers of $19^{th}$ electron in Chromium (Atomic number $= 24$) in accordance with Aufbau principle?
question: Which of the following is correct set of $4$ quantum numbers of $19^{th}$ electron in Chromium (Atomic number $= 24$) in accordance with Aufbau principle?
solution:
The atomic number of Chromium is $24$.

According to the Aufbau principle, the order of filling of electrons is $1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d$.

The electronic configuration of the first $18$ electrons is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6$.

The $19^{th}$ electron enters the $4s$ orbital.

For the $4s$ orbital, the quantum numbers are:
Principal quantum number, $n = 4$
Azimuthal quantum number, $l = 0$
Magnetic quantum number, $m = 0$
Spin quantum number, $s = +\dfrac{1}{2}$ or $-\dfrac{1}{2}$

This matches the set given in the fourth option.

Answer: $n = 4, l = 0, m = 0, s = +\dfrac{1}{2}$

question:

Charge on an electron =1.6×10-19C

solution:
De-broglie-wave length of electron:

λe=h2 m(KE)∵e-is accelerated  from rest ⇒KE=q×V

λ=h2mqv

=6.63×10-342×1.6×10-19×9.1×10-31×40×103

=0.614×10-11 m

=6.14×10-12 m

Nearest integer =6

OR

λ=12.3VÅ

=12.3200=6.15×10-12 m

Ans. is 6

subject: Chemistry
q: For emission line of atomic hydrogen from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the plot of wave number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>-</mo></mover></mfenced></math> against <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mfenced></math> will be: (The Rydberg constant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></msub></math> is in wave number unit)
question: For emission line of atomic hydrogen from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the plot of wave number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>-</mo></mover></mfenced></math> against <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mfenced></math> will be: (The Rydberg constant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></msub></math> is in wave number unit)
solution:
According to Rydberg's equation,



1λ=v -=RHZ21n12-1n22

Z=1 for H atom

=RH×12(1nf2−182)

v¯=RHnf2−RH64

slope, m=+RH

The curve is linear

subject: Chemistry
q: The wavelength of an electron of kinetic energy 4.50×10-29 J is ________×10–5 m. (Nearest integer) Given: mass of electron is9×10–31 kg, h=6.6×10–34 Js
question: The wavelength of an electron of kinetic energy 4.50×10-29 J is ________×10–5 m. (Nearest integer) Given: mass of electron is9×10–31 kg, h=6.6×10–34 Js
solution:
Given:
Kinetic energy(K.E.)=4.55×10−29J
Mass of electron(me)=9.1×10−31kgh=6.6×10−34J−sec

debroglie wavelength λd=hmv=h2mKE

Here λ is the de Broglie wavelength, h is Planck's constant, m is the mass of the particle, v is its velocity, and p is the momentum of the particle, which is equal to mv.

λ=h2mKE

=6.6×10-342×9×10-31×4.5×10-29=6.6×10-349×10-30=6.69×10-4

=669×10-5

=7.33×10-5

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn></math> molal aqueous solution of sucrose can be cooled up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> before freezing. The amount of ice (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) that will be separated out is ___________. (Nearest integer) [Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>86</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn></math> molal aqueous solution of sucrose can be cooled up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> before freezing. The amount of ice (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>) that will be separated out is ___________. (Nearest integer) [Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>86</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Let mass of water initially present =xg

Mass of sucrose =(1000-x)g

Number of moles sucrose =1000-x342

Molality M=1000-x342x/1000=0.75

2565.5x=106-1000x

x=795.86 g

no. of moles of sucrose=0.5969

ΔTf=ikf m

4=1×1.86×0.5969(795.86-a)g×1000

Ice separated=518.3 g
